dat110-q-073.explanationIncorrect: Parallel writing on all replicas (without coordination) or on a random replica does not give consistency; minority writing violates the quorum principles.
optionExplanations: Correct: a single primary serializes writes; backups follow. The standard primary-backup model.
That is active replication or multi-master, not primary-based.
Writing to an arbitrary replica allows conflicts; that is not what the primary-based model offers.
Minority writing does not give durable consistency — it breaks the quorum overlap.

dat110-q-074.question: What is the main problem with reliable multicast that uses direct feedback messages (ACKs) from all receivers?
options: Feedback implosion — the sender is overwhelmed by simultaneous ACKs from many receivers
ACKs arrive out of order and must be sorted in a queue on the sender side
Receivers cannot distinguish reliable from unreliable multicast messages
The sender loses the original multicast message after the first retransmission
dat110-q-074.explanationCorrect: When many receivers all send ACKs at the same time, the sender node is overwhelmed (feedback implosion). Solutions include hierarchical feedback (local aggregators) or NACK-based feedback only on loss.
dat110-q-074.explanationIncorrect: ACK ordering and loss of the sender's message are not the core problem; the volume of feedback is.
optionExplanations: Correct: ACKs from N receivers scale poorly — feedback implosion is the classic problem.
Ordering of ACKs is trivial; the volume is the problem, not the order.
Multicast reliability is a property of the sender protocol, not something receivers "distinguish" between.
The sender keeps the message in a buffer until it is ACKed — this is not a loss problem.

dat110-q-075.question: What is a general, widely used technique for achieving process resilience in distributed systems?
options: Organize several identical processes in a group so that some can take over on failure
Remove the fault source by static analysis of the source code beforehand
Predict failures with machine learning and take action before they happen
Shut down the service at the slightest sign of failure to avoid damage
dat110-q-075.explanationCorrect: Process groups (replication) are the main technique: several identical processes run simultaneously, and if one fails, the rest can continue the service. Quorum and primary-based are subclasses.
dat110-q-075.explanationIncorrect: Static analysis and prediction help during development/operation, but they are not the core of runtime resilience; shutting down is not resilience.
optionExplanations: Correct: replicated process groups are the canonical runtime technique for fault tolerance.
Static analysis is fault avoidance, not fault tolerance. Different stages in fault management.
Prediction can complement replication, but it is not the general technique for resilience itself.
Fail-stop is a fault model, not a resilience technique — it removes availability.

dat110-q-076.question: How many processes are required in total to achieve majority-vote consensus in a leaderless protocol that tolerates k crash failures?
options: k + 1 processes — just one more than the number of possible failures
2k + 1 processes — so that the majority is always correct
3k + 1 processes — to ensure Byzantine tolerance
k × 2 processes — exactly twice as many as failures
dat110-q-076.explanationCorrect: For crash failures, 2k+1 processes suffice: if k crash, there are still k+1 (a majority) left that can make a decision. Byzantine failures, by contrast, require 3k+1 because the faulty ones can actively lie.
dat110-q-076.explanationIncorrect: k+1 is too few (the majority can be lost); 3k+1 is for Byzantine; "k×2" is just 2k, one fewer than needed.
optionExplanations: If k fail and only 1 is left, we do not have a majority to make a decision.
Correct: 2k+1 → the majority (k+1) survives the crash of k. Standard crash-tolerant consensus.
3k+1 is used for Byzantine failures where nodes can behave arbitrarily — overkill for pure crash.
2k is too few: if k fail, 2k − k = k are left — an equal number, not a majority.

dat110-q-077.question: How are IP addresses used in the network layer of the Internet architecture?
options: IP addresses are assigned to each network interface on both hosts and routers
IP addresses are assigned only to hosts; routers use MAC addresses internally
IP addresses are assigned one per host and one per router, regardless of the number of interfaces
IP addresses are assigned only to subnets as a whole, not to individual nodes
dat110-q-077.explanationCorrect: It is the network INTERFACE (not the node) that has an IP address. A router with three interfaces has three IP addresses. A host with both Wi-Fi and wired has two.
dat110-q-077.explanationIncorrect: Per-host or per-router addressing is wrong; routers use IP for forwarding (not just MAC); a subnet is a network prefix, not the address itself.
optionExplanations: Correct: one IP per interface. A multi-homed host or router has several IP addresses.
Routers use IP to forward datagrams — not just MAC addresses.
Per-host addressing does not exist in the IPv4 architecture; per-interface is the norm.
A subnet prefix identifies a group of interfaces, but individual addresses are still assigned.

dat110-q-078.question: What kind of information is found in a typical forwarding table at a router?
options: Shortest paths between all pairs of routers in the entire network
A mapping between destination-address prefix and outgoing link
A mapping between source address and incoming link
A list of which links the router is physically connected to
dat110-q-078.explanationCorrect: The forwarding table maps destination prefixes to outgoing interfaces. The router uses longest-prefix match to decide where an incoming datagram should be forwarded.
dat110-q-078.explanationIncorrect: Global paths or source-based mappings are not standard forwarding-table contents; a link list alone does not provide enough information to forward.
optionExplanations: All-pairs shortest-path information belongs to routing algorithms (the control plane), not the per-packet forwarding table.
Correct: (destination prefix → outgoing link). Used with longest-prefix match.
Forwarding is determined by the DESTINATION, not the source. Source routing is a rare special variant.
Links are the PHYSICAL aspect; forwarding requires a logical mapping from addresses to links.

dat110-q-079.question: How many IP addresses does the subnet 223.1.1.0/23 contain?
options: 128 addresses — 2^7 values under /25
256 addresses — one octet left open
512 addresses — two octets under the /23 mask boundary
1024 addresses — 2^10 values under /22
dat110-q-079.explanationCorrect: /23 means 23 fixed prefix bits and 32 − 23 = 9 host bits. 2^9 = 512 addresses in the block. Two /24 blocks packed together.
dat110-q-079.explanationIncorrect: A common trap is to read /23 as /24 (256) or to confuse it with other prefix lengths; remember the formula 2^(32 − prefix).
optionExplanations: /25 would give 128 addresses. /23 is a much larger block.
256 belongs to /24. /23 is exactly twice as large.
Correct: 2^(32−23) = 2^9 = 512 addresses.
1024 belongs to /22 (a prefix one bit shorter).

dat110-q-080.question: What is DHCP (Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol) used for?
options: Mapping host names to IP addresses via queries to a name server
Exchanging routing information between routers in the same autonomous system
Automatically configuring a host's network interface with an IP address and settings
Discovering the MAC address belonging to a given IP address on the same subnet
dat110-q-080.explanationCorrect: DHCP lets a host be assigned an IP address, subnet mask, default gateway, and DNS server address dynamically at startup, without manual configuration.
dat110-q-080.explanationIncorrect: Host→IP is DNS; routing information is routing protocols (RIP, OSPF); IP→MAC is ARP.
optionExplanations: It is DNS that maps names to IP, not DHCP.
Routing protocols (RIP/OSPF/BGP) share routing information; DHCP is host configuration.
Correct: dynamic host configuration — IP, mask, gateway, DNS. The classic DHCP role.
IP→MAC resolution is ARP, not DHCP.

dat110-q-081.question: In a network with 10 routers — how many Dijkstra runs are needed for each individual router to find its least-cost paths to all the others?
options: 1 run — centrally on a configuration server
10 runs — one per router, each computing its own shortest paths
3 runs — one per network segment in the topology
100 runs — one per (source, destination) pair in a full mesh
dat110-q-081.explanationCorrect: Each router must run Dijkstra with itself as the source to build its local forwarding table. With 10 routers that gives 10 runs (one per router).
dat110-q-081.explanationIncorrect: A centralized approach is not the standard Link-State model (distributed Dijkstra); per-pair is O(N^2) and redundant.
optionExplanations: In Link-State protocols, each router runs Dijkstra LOCALLY after receiving the topology.
Correct: one Dijkstra run per router = 10 runs for 10 routers.
The number of runs is determined by the number of routers, not the number of segments.
Each Dijkstra run from a single source finds paths to ALL destinations — not per pair.

dat110-q-082.question: Which service does the link layer provide?
options: Unreliable transmission of frames over a single communication link
Reliable multi-hop forwarding of frames between hosts
A reliable end-to-end byte stream between application processes
Routing computation across multiple subnet boundaries
dat110-q-082.explanationCorrect: The link layer handles transmission of frames over a single link (the same broadcast domain). Reliability can be offered by some link-layer protocols (WiFi does this), but it is not universal.
dat110-q-082.explanationIncorrect: Multi-hop is the network layer; an end-to-end byte stream is TCP; routing belongs to the network layer's control plane.
optionExplanations: Correct: link layer = a single link, frame-oriented, usually best-effort (Ethernet).
Multi-hop is the network layer. The link layer only knows about the single physical link.
A byte stream is provided by the transport layer (TCP), not the link layer.
Routing is network-layer functionality — the link layer does not care about subnets.

dat110-q-083.question: What is the purpose of the Address Resolution Protocol (ARP)?
options: Finding the MAC address of a host or router that has a given IP address on the same subnet
Assigning an IP address to a network interface at startup
Assigning a MAC address to a network card during manufacturing
Translating domain names to IP addresses via a query to a name server
dat110-q-083.explanationCorrect: ARP sends out a broadcast on the local subnet asking "who has IP X?". The owner replies with its MAC. The result is cached locally in the ARP table.
dat110-q-083.explanationIncorrect: IP assignment is DHCP; the MAC is set at the factory; name → IP is DNS.
optionExplanations: Correct: ARP resolves IP → MAC within a single broadcast domain.
Dynamic IP assignment is DHCP. ARP assumes the IP is already known.
MAC addresses are burned into the network card by the manufacturer, not via a protocol.
DNS does name → IP. ARP is one step lower (IP → MAC).

dat110-q-084.question: What kind of information is stored in a host's ARP table?
options: The association between IP addresses and MAC addresses for nodes on the same subnet
The association between port numbers and process ids for open sockets
The association between interface number and MAC address for the host's own cards
A list of which hosts are to be assigned which IP addresses at startup
dat110-q-084.explanationCorrect: The ARP table is a cache that stores (IP, MAC, TTL) triples. Hosts look up here before sending; on a cache miss an ARP query is triggered.
dat110-q-084.explanationIncorrect: Port-PID mapping is the OS sockets table; interface→MAC is static local information; IP assignment is the DHCP server's domain.
optionExplanations: Correct: an (IP → MAC) cache for the local subnet, with a time limit.
Port↔PID is OS-internal socket state, not the ARP domain.
A local interface → MAC mapping is not the ARP table; it is host configuration.
IP assignment resides on the DHCP server, not in the client's ARP table.

dat110-q-085.question: On which type of communication link is a link access protocol needed to coordinate access?
options: Broadcast link — where multiple nodes share the same medium
Point-to-point link — where exactly two nodes are connected together
Optical fiber link — regardless of the number of endpoints
Wireless link — only because it uses radio frequencies
dat110-q-085.explanationCorrect: On broadcast links (Ethernet hub, WiFi, satellite) multiple nodes can attempt to send at the same time. A MAC protocol (CSMA/CD, CSMA/CA, TDMA, FDMA) is needed to coordinate and avoid/handle collisions.
dat110-q-085.explanationIncorrect: Point-to-point needs no coordination (only two parties); the fiber/wireless distinction is physical, not logical if there are only two endpoints.
optionExplanations: Correct: shared medium → possible concurrency → a MAC protocol is needed.
With only two parties there is no collision risk; full-duplex or turn-taking suffices.
Fiber can be both point-to-point and shared; the topology decides, not the medium.
Wireless needs MAC because it is broadcast — not because it is radio per se.
